summary:
The Nine Years' War (1688–1697 AD), also called the War of the Grand Alliance or the War of the League of Augsburg, was a major European conflict in which [[event:2361|Louis XIV's]] France fought against a broad coalition including England (post-[[event:2330|Glorious Revolution]]), the Dutch Republic, the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, Savoy, and Portugal — one of the most extensive coalitions ever assembled against a single power. The war arose from Louis's continued aggressive expansion: his devastation of the Palatinate (the 'burning of the Palatinate,' 1688–1689) shocked European opinion, and his support for the deposed James II against William III brought England firmly into the coalition. France, though fighting on multiple fronts, proved remarkably resilient — the Battle of Fleurus (1690) and other victories demonstrated continued French military effectiveness.

The Nine Years' War was the first major conflict to extend significantly to the Americas and India, establishing the pattern of simultaneous European and colonial warfare that would characterize the 18th century. King William's War (the North American theater) saw English and French colonists fighting along the frontier from Acadia to New England, with Indigenous allies on both sides. The war's enormous cost — France mobilized nearly 400,000 soldiers — exhausted both sides and accelerated fiscal crises across Europe. The [[event:3503|Treaty of Ryswick]] (1697) ended the war with France making significant territorial concessions, though the underlying Franco-British-Dutch rivalry remained unresolved and would resume almost immediately in the [[event:2347|War of the Spanish Succession]] (1701–1714). The Nine Years' War is also significant as the context in which [[event:2511|the Bank of England]] was founded (1694), as England sought innovative methods to finance prolonged warfare.
